questions.prompt: What does the @Test annotation do in TestNG?
options: It marks a method as a test case that TestNG finds and runs on its own
It deletes the method after one run
It turns the method into the program's main entry point
It makes the method run only when called from another test
questions.explanation: @Test tells TestNG the method is a test. The framework discovers every @Test method, runs each in isolation, and reports its result — you never invoke them yourself.

questions.prompt: Why does a test method with no assertion prove nothing?
options: TestNG refuses to run methods without assertions
Without a check that can fail, the test always passes regardless of the actual behaviour
Assertions are the only way to open a browser
An assertion is what names the test method
questions.explanation: An assertion is the verdict. With nothing that can fail, the method runs to the end and reports pass even when the behaviour is wrong, so it verifies nothing.

questions.prompt: In assertEquals(actual, expected), what happens and why does argument order matter?
options: It passes only when actual is null; order changes nothing
It fails when the two differ, and order sets which value the failure message labels 'expected'
It always passes but logs a warning if they differ
It compares the two and order is purely cosmetic with no effect on output
questions.explanation: assertEquals fails the test when actual and expected differ. The order drives the message ('expected X but found Y'), so swapping the arguments produces a misleading report.

questions.prompt: What is the key difference between a hard assert and a SoftAssert?
options: A hard assert stops the test on failure; a SoftAssert collects failures and reports them at assertAll()
A SoftAssert stops the test instantly; a hard assert ignores failures
A hard assert only works in the main method
There is no difference; the names are interchangeable
questions.explanation: A hard assert halts the test the moment it fails. A SoftAssert records each failure and only reports them when you call assertAll(), letting one test check several things — but forgetting assertAll() makes it pass silently.
